21	def prepare_input(x, y, axis, dydx=None, xp=None):
22	    """Prepare input for cubic spline interpolators.
23	
24	    All data are converted to numpy arrays and checked for correctness.
25	    Axes equal to `axis` of arrays `y` and `dydx` are moved to be the 0th
26	    axis. The value of `axis` is converted to lie in
27	    [0, number of dimensions of `y`).
28	    """
29	
30	    x, y = map(xp.asarray, (x, y))
31	    if xp.isdtype(x.dtype, "complex floating"):
32	        raise ValueError("`x` must contain real values.")
33	    x = xp.astype(x, xp.float64)
34	
35	    if xp.isdtype(y.dtype, "complex floating"):
36	        dtype = xp.complex128
37	    else:
38	        dtype = xp.float64
39	
40	    if dydx is not None:
41	        dydx = xp.asarray(dydx)
42	        if y.shape != dydx.shape:
43	            raise ValueError("The shapes of `y` and `dydx` must be identical.")
44	        if xp.isdtype(dydx.dtype, "complex floating"):
45	            dtype = xp.complex128
46	        dydx = xp.astype(dydx, dtype, copy=False)
47	
48	    y = xp.astype(y, dtype, copy=False)
49	    axis = axis % y.ndim
50	    if x.ndim != 1:
51	        raise ValueError("`x` must be 1-dimensional.")
52	    if x.shape[0] < 2:
53	        raise ValueError("`x` must contain at least 2 elements.")
54	    if x.shape[0] != y.shape[axis]:
55	        raise ValueError(f"The length of `y` along `axis`={axis} doesn't "

57

59	        raise ValueError("`x` must contain only finite values.")

63	    if dydx is not None and not xp.all(xp.isfinite(dydx)):

86	    """Piecewise cubic interpolator to fit values and first derivatives (C1 smooth).
87	
88	    The result is represented as a `PPoly` instance.
89	
90	    Parameters
91	    ----------
92	    x : array_like, shape (n,)
93	        1-D array containing values of the independent variable.
94	        Values must be real, finite and in strictly increasing order.
95	    y : array_like
96	        Array containing values of the dependent variable. It can have
97	        arbitrary number of dimensions, but the length along ``axis``
98	        (see below) must match the length of ``x``. Values must be finite.
99	    dydx : array_like
100	        Array containing derivatives of the dependent variable. It can have
101	        arbitrary number of dimensions, but the length along ``axis``
102	        (see below) must match the length of ``x``. Values must be finite.
103	    axis : int, optional
104	        Axis along which `y` is assumed to be varying. Meaning that for
105	        ``x[i]`` the corresponding values are ``np.take(y, i, axis=axis)``.
106	        Default is 0.
107	    extrapolate : {bool, 'periodic', None}, optional
108	        If bool, determines whether to extrapolate to out-of-bounds points
109	        based on first and last intervals, or to return NaNs. If 'periodic',
110	        periodic extrapolation is used. If None (default), it is set to True.
111	        See :ref:`tutorial-interpolate_out_of_bounds`.
112	
113	    Attributes
114	    ----------
115	    x : ndarray, shape (n,)
116	        Breakpoints. The same ``x`` which was passed to the constructor.
117	    c : ndarray, shape (4, n-1, ...)
118	        Coefficients of the polynomials on each segment. The trailing
119	        dimensions match the dimensions of `y`, excluding ``axis``.
120	        For example, if `y` is 1-D, then ``c[k, i]`` is a coefficient for
121	        ``(x-x[i])**(3-k)`` on the segment between ``x[i]`` and ``x[i+1]``.
122	    axis : int
123	        Interpolation axis. The same axis which was passed to the
124	        constructor.
125	
126	    Methods
127	    -------
128	    __call__
129	    derivative
130	    antiderivative
131	    integrate
132	    solve
133	    roots
134	
135	    See Also
136	    --------
137	    Akima1DInterpolator : Akima 1D interpolator.
138	    PchipInterpolator : PCHIP 1-D monotonic cubic interpolator.
139	    CubicSpline : Cubic spline data interpolator.
140	    PPoly : Piecewise polynomial in terms of coefficients and breakpoints
141	
142	    Notes
143	    -----
144	    If you want to create a higher-order spline matching higher-order
145	    derivatives, use `BPoly.from_derivatives`.
146	
147	    References
148	    ----------
149	    .. [1] `Cubic Hermite spline
150	            <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cubic_Hermite_spline>`_
151	            on Wikipedia.
152	    """

154	    def __init__(self, x, y, dydx, axis=0, extrapolate=None):
155	        xp = array_namespace(x, y, dydx)

159

161

163	        slope = xp.diff(y, axis=0) / dxr
164	        t = (dydx[:-1, ...] + dydx[1:, ...] - 2 * slope) / dxr
165	
166	        c = xp.stack((
167	           t / dxr,
168	           (slope - dydx[:-1, ...]) / dxr - t,
169	           dydx[:-1, ...],
170	           y[:-1, ...]
171	        ))

173	        super().__init__(c, x, extrapolate=extrapolate)

185	    r"""PCHIP shape-preserving interpolator (C1 smooth).
186	
187	    ``x`` and ``y`` are arrays of values used to approximate some function f,
188	    with ``y = f(x)``. The interpolant uses monotonic cubic splines
189	    to find the value of new points. (PCHIP stands for Piecewise Cubic
190	    Hermite Interpolating Polynomial).
191	
192	    Parameters
193	    ----------
194	    x : ndarray, shape (npoints, )
195	        A 1-D array of monotonically increasing real values. ``x`` cannot
196	        include duplicate values (otherwise f is overspecified)
197	    y : ndarray, shape (..., npoints, ...)
198	        An N-D array of real values. ``y``'s length along the interpolation
199	        axis must be equal to the length of ``x``. Use the ``axis``
200	        parameter to select the interpolation axis.
201	    axis : int, optional
202	        Axis in the ``y`` array corresponding to the x-coordinate values. Defaults
203	        to ``axis=0``.
204	    extrapolate : {bool, 'periodic', None}, optional
205	        If bool, determines whether to extrapolate to out-of-bounds points
206	        based on first and last intervals, or to return NaNs. If 'periodic',
207	        periodic extrapolation is used. If None (default), it is set to True.
208	        See :ref:`tutorial-interpolate_out_of_bounds`.
209	
210	    Methods
211	    -------
212	    __call__
213	    derivative
214	    antiderivative
215	    integrate
216	    solve
217	    roots
218	
219	
220	    See Also
221	    --------
222	    CubicHermiteSpline : Piecewise-cubic interpolator.
223	    Akima1DInterpolator : Akima 1D interpolator.
224	    CubicSpline : Cubic spline data interpolator.
225	    PPoly : Piecewise polynomial in terms of coefficients and breakpoints.
226	
227	    Notes
228	    -----
229	    The interpolator preserves monotonicity in the interpolation data and does
230	    not overshoot if the data is not smooth.
231	
232	    The first derivatives are guaranteed to be continuous, but the second
233	    derivatives may jump at :math:`x_k`.
234	
235	    Determines the derivatives at the points :math:`x_k`, :math:`f'_k`,
236	    by using PCHIP algorithm [1]_.
237	
238	    Let :math:`h_k = x_{k+1} - x_k`, and  :math:`d_k = (y_{k+1} - y_k) / h_k`
239	    are the slopes at internal points :math:`x_k`.
240	    If the signs of :math:`d_k` and :math:`d_{k-1}` are different or either of
241	    them equals zero, then :math:`f'_k = 0`. Otherwise, it is given by the
242	    weighted harmonic mean
243	
244	    .. math::
245	
246	        \frac{w_1 + w_2}{f'_k} = \frac{w_1}{d_{k-1}} + \frac{w_2}{d_k}
247	
248	    where :math:`w_1 = 2 h_k + h_{k-1}` and :math:`w_2 = h_k + 2 h_{k-1}`.
249	
250	    The end slopes are set using a one-sided scheme [2]_.
251	
252	
253	    References
254	    ----------
255	    .. [1] F. N. Fritsch and J. Butland,
256	           A method for constructing local
257	           monotone piecewise cubic interpolants,
258	           SIAM J. Sci. Comput., 5(2), 300-304 (1984).
259	           :doi:`10.1137/0905021`.
260	    .. [2] C. Moler, Numerical Computing with Matlab, 2004.
261	           :doi:`10.1137/1.9780898717952`
262	
263	    """
264	
265	    # PchipInterpolator is not generic in scipy-stubs
266	    __class_getitem__ = None  # type:ignore[assignment]
267	
268	    def __init__(self, x, y, axis=0, extrapolate=None):
269	        xp = array_namespace(x, y)
270	        x, _, y, axis, _ = prepare_input(x, y, axis, xp=xp)
271	        if xp.isdtype(y.dtype, "complex floating"):
272	            msg = ("`PchipInterpolator` only works with real values for `y`. "
273	                   "If you are trying to use the real components of the passed array, "
274	                   "use `np.real` on the array before passing to `PchipInterpolator`.")
275	            raise ValueError(msg)
276	        xv = xp.reshape(x, (x.shape[0],) + (1,)*(y.ndim-1))
277	        dk = self._find_derivatives(xv, y, xp=xp)
278	        super().__init__(x, y, dk, axis=0, extrapolate=extrapolate)
279	        self.axis = axis

281	    @staticmethod
282	    def _edge_case(h0, h1, m0, m1, xp):
283	        # one-sided three-point estimate for the derivative
284	        d = ((2*h0 + h1)*m0 - h0*m1) / (h0 + h1)
285	
286	        # try to preserve shape
287	        mask = xp.sign(d) != xp.sign(m0)
288	        mask2 = (xp.sign(m0) != xp.sign(m1)) & (xp.abs(d) > 3.*xp.abs(m0))
289	        mmm = (~mask) & mask2
290	
291	        d = xpx.at(d)[mask].set(0.)
292	        d = xpx.at(d)[mmm].set(3.*m0[mmm])
293	
294	        return d
295	
296	    @staticmethod
297	    def _find_derivatives(x, y, xp):
298	        # Determine the derivatives at the points y_k, d_k, by using
299	        #  PCHIP algorithm is:
300	        # We choose the derivatives at the point x_k by
301	        # Let m_k be the slope of the kth segment (between k and k+1)
302	        # If m_k=0 or m_{k-1}=0 or sgn(m_k) != sgn(m_{k-1}) then d_k == 0
303	        # else use weighted harmonic mean:
304	        #   w_1 = 2h_k + h_{k-1}, w_2 = h_k + 2h_{k-1}
305	        #   1/d_k = 1/(w_1 + w_2)*(w_1 / m_k + w_2 / m_{k-1})
306	        #   where h_k is the spacing between x_k and x_{k+1}
307	        y_shape = y.shape
308	        if y.ndim == 1:
309	            # So that _edge_case doesn't end up assigning to scalars
310	            x = x[:, None]
311	            y = y[:, None]
312	
313	        hk = x[1:, ...] - x[:-1, ...]
314	        mk = (y[1:, ...] - y[:-1, ...]) / hk
315	
316	        if y.shape[0] == 2:
317	            # edge case: only have two points, use linear interpolation
318	            dk = xp.zeros_like(y)
319	            dk = xpx.at(dk)[0, ...].set(mk[0, ...])
320	            dk = xpx.at(dk)[1, ...].set(mk[0, ...])
321	            return xp.reshape(dk, y_shape)
322	
323	        smk = xp.sign(mk)
324	        condition = ((smk[1:, ...] != smk[:-1, ...])
325	                     | (mk[1:, ...] == 0) | (mk[:-1, ...] == 0))
326	
327	        w1 = 2*hk[1:, ...] + hk[:-1, ...]
328	        w2 = hk[1:, ...] + 2*hk[:-1, ...]
329	
330	        # values where division by zero occurs will be excluded
331	        # by 'condition' afterwards
332	        with np.errstate(divide='ignore', invalid='ignore'):
333	            whmean = (w1/mk[:-1, ...] + w2/mk[1:, ...]) / (w1 + w2)
334	
335	            dk = xp.zeros_like(y)
336	            dk = xpx.at(dk)[1:-1, ...].set(
337	                xp.where(condition, xp.zeros_like(whmean), 1.0 / whmean)
338	            )
339	
340	        # special case endpoints, as suggested in
341	        # Cleve Moler, Numerical Computing with MATLAB, Chap 3.6 (pchiptx.m)
342	        dk = xpx.at(dk)[0, ...].set(
343	            PchipInterpolator._edge_case(
344	                hk[0, ...], hk[1, ...], mk[0, ...], mk[1, ...], xp=xp
345	            )
346	        )
347	        dk = xpx.at(dk)[-1, ...].set(
348	            PchipInterpolator._edge_case(
349	                hk[-1, ...], hk[-2, ...], mk[-1, ...], mk[-2, ...], xp=xp
350	            )
351	        )
352	
353	        return xp.reshape(dk, y_shape)

356	def pchip_interpolate(xi, yi, x, der=0, axis=0):
357	    """
358	    Convenience function for pchip interpolation.
359	
360	    xi and yi are arrays of values used to approximate some function f,
361	    with ``yi = f(xi)``. The interpolant uses monotonic cubic splines
362	    to find the value of new points x and the derivatives there.
363	
364	    See `scipy.interpolate.PchipInterpolator` for details.
365	
366	    Parameters
367	    ----------
368	    xi : array_like
369	        A sorted list of x-coordinates, of length N.
370	    yi : array_like
371	        A 1-D array of real values. `yi`'s length along the interpolation
372	        axis must be equal to the length of `xi`. If N-D array, use axis
373	        parameter to select correct axis.
374	    x : scalar or array_like
375	        Of length M.
376	    der : int or list, optional
377	        Derivatives to extract. The 0th derivative can be included to
378	        return the function value.
379	    axis : int, optional
380	        Axis in the yi array corresponding to the x-coordinate values.
381	
382	    Returns
383	    -------
384	    y : scalar or array_like
385	        The result, of length R or length M or M by R.
386	
387	    See Also
388	    --------
389	    PchipInterpolator : PCHIP 1-D monotonic cubic interpolator.
390	
391	    Examples
392	    --------
393	    We can interpolate 2D observed data using pchip interpolation:
394	
395	    >>> import numpy as np
396	    >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
397	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import pchip_interpolate
398	    >>> x_observed = np.linspace(0.0, 10.0, 11)
399	    >>> y_observed = np.sin(x_observed)
400	    >>> x = np.linspace(min(x_observed), max(x_observed), num=100)
401	    >>> y = pchip_interpolate(x_observed, y_observed, x)
402	    >>> plt.plot(x_observed, y_observed, "o", label="observation")
403	    >>> plt.plot(x, y, label="pchip interpolation")
404	    >>> plt.legend()
405	    >>> plt.show()
406	
407	    """
408	    P = PchipInterpolator(xi, yi, axis=axis)
409	
410	    if der == 0:
411	        return P(x)
412	    elif _isscalar(der):

426	class Akima1DInterpolator(CubicHermiteSpline):
427	    r"""Akima "visually pleasing" interpolator (C1 smooth).
428	
429	    Fit piecewise cubic polynomials, given vectors x and y. The interpolation
430	    method by Akima uses a continuously differentiable sub-spline built from
431	    piecewise cubic polynomials. The resultant curve passes through the given
432	    data points and will appear smooth and natural.
433	
434	    Parameters
435	    ----------
436	    x : ndarray, shape (npoints, )
437	        1-D array of monotonically increasing real values.
438	    y : ndarray, shape (..., npoints, ...)
439	        N-D array of real values. The length of ``y`` along the interpolation axis
440	        must be equal to the length of ``x``. Use the ``axis`` parameter to
441	        select the interpolation axis.
442	    axis : int, optional
443	        Axis in the ``y`` array corresponding to the x-coordinate values. Defaults
444	        to ``axis=0``.
445	    method : {'akima', 'makima'}, optional
446	        If ``"makima"``, use the modified Akima interpolation [2]_.
447	        Defaults to ``"akima"``, use the Akima interpolation [1]_.
448	
449	        .. versionadded:: 1.13.0
450	
451	    extrapolate : {bool, 'periodic', None}, optional
452	        If bool, determines whether to extrapolate to out-of-bounds points
453	        based on first and last intervals, or to return NaNs. If 'periodic',
454	        periodic extrapolation is used. If None (default), it is set to False.
455	        See :ref:`tutorial-interpolate_out_of_bounds`.
456	
457	    Methods
458	    -------
459	    __call__
460	    derivative
461	    antiderivative
462	    integrate
463	    solve
464	    roots
465	
466	    See Also
467	    --------
468	    PchipInterpolator : PCHIP 1-D monotonic cubic interpolator.
469	    CubicSpline : Cubic spline data interpolator.
470	    PPoly : Piecewise polynomial in terms of coefficients and breakpoints
471	
472	    Notes
473	    -----
474	    .. versionadded:: 0.14
475	
476	    Use only for precise data, as the fitted curve passes through the given
477	    points exactly. This routine is useful for plotting a pleasingly smooth
478	    curve through a few given points for purposes of plotting.
479	
480	    Let :math:`\delta_i = (y_{i+1} - y_i) / (x_{i+1} - x_i)` be the slopes of
481	    the interval :math:`\left[x_i, x_{i+1}\right)`. Akima's derivative at
482	    :math:`x_i` is defined as:
483	
484	    .. math::
485	
486	        d_i = \frac{w_1}{w_1 + w_2}\delta_{i-1} + \frac{w_2}{w_1 + w_2}\delta_i
487	
488	    In the Akima interpolation [1]_ (``method="akima"``), the weights are:
489	
490	    .. math::
491	
492	        \begin{aligned}
493	        w_1 &= |\delta_{i+1} - \delta_i| \\
494	        w_2 &= |\delta_{i-1} - \delta_{i-2}|
495	        \end{aligned}
496	
497	    In the modified Akima interpolation [2]_ (``method="makima"``),
498	    to eliminate overshoot and avoid edge cases of both numerator and
499	    denominator being equal to 0, the weights are modified as follows:
500	
501	    .. math::
502	
503	        \begin{align*}
504	        w_1 &= |\delta_{i+1} - \delta_i| + |\delta_{i+1} + \delta_i| / 2 \\
505	        w_2 &= |\delta_{i-1} - \delta_{i-2}| + |\delta_{i-1} + \delta_{i-2}| / 2
506	        \end{align*}
507	
508	    Examples
509	    --------
510	    Comparison of ``method="akima"`` and ``method="makima"``:
511	
512	    >>> import numpy as np
513	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import Akima1DInterpolator
514	    >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
515	    >>> x = np.linspace(1, 7, 7)
516	    >>> y = np.array([-1, -1, -1, 0, 1, 1, 1])
517	    >>> xs = np.linspace(min(x), max(x), num=100)
518	    >>> y_akima = Akima1DInterpolator(x, y, method="akima")(xs)
519	    >>> y_makima = Akima1DInterpolator(x, y, method="makima")(xs)
520	
521	    >>> fig, ax = plt.subplots()
522	    >>> ax.plot(x, y, "o", label="data")
523	    >>> ax.plot(xs, y_akima, label="akima")
524	    >>> ax.plot(xs, y_makima, label="makima")
525	    >>> ax.legend()
526	    >>> fig.show()
527	
528	    The overshoot that occurred in ``"akima"`` has been avoided in ``"makima"``.
529	
530	    References
531	    ----------
532	    .. [1] A new method of interpolation and smooth curve fitting based
533	           on local procedures. Hiroshi Akima, J. ACM, October 1970, 17(4),
534	           589-602. :doi:`10.1145/321607.321609`
535	    .. [2] Makima Piecewise Cubic Interpolation. Cleve Moler and Cosmin Ionita, 2019.
536	           https://blogs.mathworks.com/cleve/2019/04/29/makima-piecewise-cubic-interpolation/
537	
538	    """
539	
540	    # PchipInterpolator is not generic in scipy-stubs
541	    __class_getitem__ = None  # type:ignore[assignment]
542	
543	    def __init__(self, x, y, axis=0, *, method: Literal["akima", "makima"]="akima",
544	                 extrapolate:bool | None = None):
545	        if method not in {"akima", "makima"}:
546	            raise NotImplementedError(f"`method`={method} is unsupported.")
547	        # Original implementation in MATLAB by N. Shamsundar (BSD licensed), see
548	        # https://www.mathworks.com/matlabcentral/fileexchange/1814-akima-interpolation
549	
550	        xp = array_namespace(x, y)
551	        x, dx, y, axis, _ = prepare_input(x, y, axis, xp=xp)
552	
553	        if xp.isdtype(y.dtype, "complex floating"):
554	            msg = ("`Akima1DInterpolator` only works with real values for `y`. "
555	                   "If you are trying to use the real components of the passed array, "
556	                   "use `np.real` on the array before passing to "
557	                   "`Akima1DInterpolator`.")
558	            raise ValueError(msg)
559	
560	        # Akima extrapolation historically False; parent class defaults to True.
561	        extrapolate = False if extrapolate is None else extrapolate
562	
563	        if y.shape[0] == 2:
564	            # edge case: only have two points, use linear interpolation
565	            xv = xp.reshape(x, (x.shape[0],) + (1,)*(y.ndim-1))
566	            hk = xv[1:, ...] - xv[:-1, ...]
567	            mk = (y[1:, ...] - y[:-1, ...]) / hk
568	            t = xp.zeros_like(y)
569	            t = xpx.at(t)[...].set(mk)
570	        else:
571	            # determine slopes between breakpoints
572	            m = xp.empty((x.shape[0] + 3, ) + y.shape[1:], dtype=x.dtype)
573	            dx = dx[(slice(None), ) + (None, ) * (y.ndim - 1)]
574	            m = xpx.at(m)[2:-2, ...].set(xp.diff(y, axis=0) / dx)
575	
576	            # add two additional points on the left ...
577	            m = xpx.at(m)[1, ...].set(2. * m[2, ...] - m[3, ...])
578	            m = xpx.at(m)[0, ...].set(2. * m[1, ...] - m[2, ...])
579	            # ... and on the right
580	            m = xpx.at(m)[-2, ...].set(2. * m[-3, ...] - m[-4, ...])
581	            m = xpx.at(m)[-1, ...].set(2. * m[-2, ...] - m[-3, ...])
582	
583	            # if m1 == m2 != m3 == m4, the slope at the breakpoint is not
584	            # defined. This is the fill value:
585	            t = .5 * (m[3:, ...] + m[:-3, ...])
586	            # get the denominator of the slope t
587	            dm = xp.abs(xp.diff(m, axis=0))

590	                f1 = dm[2:, ...] + 0.5 * pm[2:, ...]

594	                f2 = dm[:-2, ...]

611	                ind,
612	                m[1:-2, ...]
613	                + (f2 / xp.where(ind, f12, 1)) * (m[2:-1, ...] - m[1:-2, ...]),

647	class CubicSpline(CubicHermiteSpline):
648	    """Piecewise cubic interpolator to fit values (C2 smooth).
649	
650	    Interpolate data with a piecewise cubic polynomial which is twice
651	    continuously differentiable [1]_. The result is represented as a `PPoly`
652	    instance with breakpoints matching the given data.
653	
654	    Parameters
655	    ----------
656	    x : array_like, shape (n,)
657	        1-D array containing values of the independent variable.
658	        Values must be real, finite and in strictly increasing order.
659	    y : array_like
660	        Array containing values of the dependent variable. It can have
661	        arbitrary number of dimensions, but the length along ``axis``
662	        (see below) must match the length of ``x``. Values must be finite.
663	    axis : int, optional
664	        Axis along which `y` is assumed to be varying. Meaning that for
665	        ``x[i]`` the corresponding values are ``np.take(y, i, axis=axis)``.
666	        Default is 0.
667	    bc_type : str or 2-tuple, optional
668	        Boundary condition type. Two additional equations, given by the
669	        boundary conditions, are required to determine all coefficients of
670	        polynomials on each segment [2]_.
671	
672	        If `bc_type` is a string, then the specified condition will be applied
673	        at both ends of a spline. Available conditions are:
674	
675	        * 'not-a-knot' (default): The first and second segment at a curve end
676	          are the same polynomial. It is a good default when there is no
677	          information on boundary conditions.
678	        * 'periodic': The interpolated functions is assumed to be periodic
679	          of period ``x[-1] - x[0]``. The first and last value of `y` must be
680	          identical: ``y[0] == y[-1]``. This boundary condition will result in
681	          ``y'[0] == y'[-1]`` and ``y''[0] == y''[-1]``.
682	        * 'clamped': The first derivative at curves ends are zero. Assuming
683	          a 1D `y`, ``bc_type=((1, 0.0), (1, 0.0))`` is the same condition.
684	        * 'natural': The second derivative at curve ends are zero. Assuming
685	          a 1D `y`, ``bc_type=((2, 0.0), (2, 0.0))`` is the same condition.
686	
687	        If `bc_type` is a 2-tuple, the first and the second value will be
688	        applied at the curve start and end respectively. The tuple values can
689	        be one of the previously mentioned strings (except 'periodic') or a
690	        tuple ``(order, deriv_values)`` allowing to specify arbitrary
691	        derivatives at curve ends:
692	
693	        * `order`: the derivative order, 1 or 2.
694	        * `deriv_value`: array_like containing derivative values, shape must
695	          be the same as `y`, excluding ``axis`` dimension. For example, if
696	          `y` is 1-D, then `deriv_value` must be a scalar. If `y` is 3-D with
697	          the shape (n0, n1, n2) and axis=2, then `deriv_value` must be 2-D
698	          and have the shape (n0, n1).
699	    extrapolate : {bool, 'periodic', None}, optional
700	        If bool, determines whether to extrapolate to out-of-bounds points
701	        based on first and last intervals, or to return NaNs. If 'periodic',
702	        periodic extrapolation is used. If None (default), ``extrapolate`` is
703	        set to 'periodic' for ``bc_type='periodic'`` and to True otherwise.
704	        See :ref:`tutorial-interpolate_out_of_bounds`.
705	
706	    Attributes
707	    ----------
708	    x : ndarray, shape (n,)
709	        Breakpoints. The same ``x`` which was passed to the constructor.
710	    c : ndarray, shape (4, n-1, ...)
711	        Coefficients of the polynomials on each segment. The trailing
712	        dimensions match the dimensions of `y`, excluding ``axis``.
713	        For example, if `y` is 1-d, then ``c[k, i]`` is a coefficient for
714	        ``(x-x[i])**(3-k)`` on the segment between ``x[i]`` and ``x[i+1]``.
715	    axis : int
716	        Interpolation axis. The same axis which was passed to the
717	        constructor.
718	
719	    Methods
720	    -------
721	    __call__
722	    derivative
723	    antiderivative
724	    integrate
725	    solve
726	    roots
727	
728	    See Also
729	    --------
730	    Akima1DInterpolator : Akima 1D interpolator.
731	    PchipInterpolator : PCHIP 1-D monotonic cubic interpolator.
732	    PPoly : Piecewise polynomial in terms of coefficients and breakpoints.
733	
734	    Notes
735	    -----
736	    Parameters `bc_type` and ``extrapolate`` work independently, i.e. the
737	    former controls only construction of a spline, and the latter only
738	    evaluation.
739	
740	    When a boundary condition is 'not-a-knot' and n = 2, it is replaced by
741	    a condition that the first derivative is equal to the linear interpolant
742	    slope. When both boundary conditions are 'not-a-knot' and n = 3, the
743	    solution is sought as a parabola passing through given points.
744	
745	    When 'not-a-knot' boundary conditions is applied to both ends, the
746	    resulting spline will be the same as returned by `splrep` (with ``s=0``)
747	    and `InterpolatedUnivariateSpline`, but these two methods use a
748	    representation in B-spline basis.
749	
750	    .. versionadded:: 0.18.0
751	
752	    Examples
753	    --------
754	    In this example the cubic spline is used to interpolate a sampled sinusoid.
755	    You can see that the spline continuity property holds for the first and
756	    second derivatives and violates only for the third derivative.
757	
758	    >>> import numpy as np
759	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import CubicSpline
760	    >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
761	    >>> x = np.arange(10)
762	    >>> y = np.sin(x)
763	    >>> cs = CubicSpline(x, y)
764	    >>> xs = np.arange(-0.5, 9.6, 0.1)
765	    >>> fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4))
766	    >>> ax.plot(x, y, 'o', label='data')
767	    >>> ax.plot(xs, np.sin(xs), label='true')
768	    >>> ax.plot(xs, cs(xs), label="S")
769	    >>> ax.plot(xs, cs(xs, 1), label="S'")
770	    >>> ax.plot(xs, cs(xs, 2), label="S''")
771	    >>> ax.plot(xs, cs(xs, 3), label="S'''")
772	    >>> ax.set_xlim(-0.5, 9.5)
773	    >>> ax.legend(loc='lower left', ncol=2)
774	    >>> plt.show()
775	
776	    In the second example, the unit circle is interpolated with a spline. A
777	    periodic boundary condition is used. You can see that the first derivative
778	    values, ds/dx=0, ds/dy=1 at the periodic point (1, 0) are correctly
779	    computed. Note that a circle cannot be exactly represented by a cubic
780	    spline. To increase precision, more breakpoints would be required.
781	
782	    >>> theta = 2 * np.pi * np.linspace(0, 1, 5)
783	    >>> y = np.c_[np.cos(theta), np.sin(theta)]
784	    >>> cs = CubicSpline(theta, y, bc_type='periodic')
785	    >>> print("ds/dx={:.1f} ds/dy={:.1f}".format(cs(0, 1)[0], cs(0, 1)[1]))
786	    ds/dx=0.0 ds/dy=1.0
787	    >>> xs = 2 * np.pi * np.linspace(0, 1, 100)
788	    >>> fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.5, 4))
789	    >>> ax.plot(y[:, 0], y[:, 1], 'o', label='data')
790	    >>> ax.plot(np.cos(xs), np.sin(xs), label='true')
791	    >>> ax.plot(cs(xs)[:, 0], cs(xs)[:, 1], label='spline')
792	    >>> ax.axes.set_aspect('equal')
793	    >>> ax.legend(loc='center')
794	    >>> plt.show()
795	
796	    The third example is the interpolation of a polynomial y = x**3 on the
797	    interval 0 <= x<= 1. A cubic spline can represent this function exactly.
798	    To achieve that we need to specify values and first derivatives at
799	    endpoints of the interval. Note that y' = 3 * x**2 and thus y'(0) = 0 and
800	    y'(1) = 3.
801	
802	    >>> cs = CubicSpline([0, 1], [0, 1], bc_type=((1, 0), (1, 3)))
803	    >>> x = np.linspace(0, 1)
804	    >>> np.allclose(x**3, cs(x))
805	    True
806	
807	    References
808	    ----------
809	    .. [1] `Cubic Spline Interpolation
810	            <https://en.wikiversity.org/wiki/Cubic_Spline_Interpolation>`_
811	            on Wikiversity.
812	    .. [2] Carl de Boor, "A Practical Guide to Splines", Springer-Verlag, 1978.
813	    """
814	
815	    def __init__(self, x, y, axis=0, bc_type='not-a-knot', extrapolate=None):
816	        xp = array_namespace(x, y)
817	        if is_cupy(xp):
818	            spx = scipy_namespace_for(xp)
819	            xp_cubic_spline = spx.interpolate.CubicSpline(
820	                x, y, axis=axis, bc_type=bc_type, extrapolate=extrapolate
821	            )
822	            self.__dict__.update(
823	                PPoly._construct_from_xp(xp_cubic_spline, xp_external=xp).__dict__
824	            )
825	            return
826	
827	        x, dx, y, axis, _ = prepare_input(x, y, axis, xp=np_compat)
828	        n = len(x)
829	
830	        bc, y = self._validate_bc(bc_type, y, y.shape[1:], axis)
831	
832	        if extrapolate is None:
833	            if bc[0] == 'periodic':
834	                extrapolate = 'periodic'
835	            else:
836	                extrapolate = True
837	
838	        if y.size == 0:
839	            # bail out early for zero-sized arrays
840	            s = np.zeros_like(y)
841	        else:
842	            dxr = dx.reshape([dx.shape[0]] + [1] * (y.ndim - 1))
843	            slope = np.diff(y, axis=0) / dxr
844	
845	            # If bc is 'not-a-knot' this change is just a convention.
846	            # If bc is 'periodic' then we already checked that y[0] == y[-1],
847	            # and the spline is just a constant, we handle this case in the
848	            # same way by setting the first derivatives to slope, which is 0.
849	            if n == 2:
850	                if bc[0] in ['not-a-knot', 'periodic']:
851	                    bc[0] = (1, slope[0])
852	                if bc[1] in ['not-a-knot', 'periodic']:
853	                    bc[1] = (1, slope[0])
854	
855	            # This is a special case, when both conditions are 'not-a-knot'
856	            # and n == 3. In this case 'not-a-knot' can't be handled regularly
857	            # as the both conditions are identical. We handle this case by
858	            # constructing a parabola passing through given points.
859	            if n == 3 and bc[0] == 'not-a-knot' and bc[1] == 'not-a-knot':
860	                A = np.zeros((3, 3))  # This is a standard matrix.
861	                b = np.empty((3,) + y.shape[1:], dtype=y.dtype)
862	
863	                A[0, 0] = 1
864	                A[0, 1] = 1
865	                A[1, 0] = dx[1]
866	                A[1, 1] = 2 * (dx[0] + dx[1])
867	                A[1, 2] = dx[0]
868	                A[2, 1] = 1
869	                A[2, 2] = 1
870	
871	                b[0] = 2 * slope[0]
872	                b[1] = 3 * (dxr[0] * slope[1] + dxr[1] * slope[0])
873	                b[2] = 2 * slope[1]
874	
875	                m = b.shape[0]
876	                s = solve(A, b.reshape(m, -1), overwrite_a=True, overwrite_b=True,
877	                          check_finite=False).reshape(b.shape)
878	            elif n == 3 and bc[0] == 'periodic':
879	                # In case when number of points is 3 we compute the derivatives
880	                # manually
881	                t = (slope / dxr).sum(0) / (1. / dxr).sum(0)
882	                s = np.broadcast_to(t, (n,) + y.shape[1:])
883	            else:
884	                # Find derivative values at each x[i] by solving a tridiagonal
885	                # system.
886	                A = np.zeros((3, n))  # This is a banded matrix representation.
887	                b = np.empty((n,) + y.shape[1:], dtype=y.dtype)
888	
889	                # Filling the system for i=1..n-2
890	                #                         (x[i-1] - x[i]) * s[i-1] +\
891	                # 2 * ((x[i] - x[i-1]) + (x[i+1] - x[i])) * s[i]   +\
892	                #                         (x[i] - x[i-1]) * s[i+1] =\
893	                #       3 * ((x[i+1] - x[i])*(y[i] - y[i-1])/(x[i] - x[i-1]) +\
894	                #           (x[i] - x[i-1])*(y[i+1] - y[i])/(x[i+1] - x[i]))
895	
896	                A[1, 1:-1] = 2 * (dx[:-1] + dx[1:])  # The diagonal
897	                A[0, 2:] = dx[:-1]                   # The upper diagonal
898	                A[-1, :-2] = dx[1:]                  # The lower diagonal
899	
900	                b[1:-1] = 3 * (dxr[1:] * slope[:-1] + dxr[:-1] * slope[1:])
901	
902	                bc_start, bc_end = bc
903	
904	                if bc_start == 'periodic':
905	                    # Due to the periodicity, and because y[-1] = y[0], the
906	                    # linear system has (n-1) unknowns/equations instead of n:
907	                    A = A[:, 0:-1]
908	                    A[1, 0] = 2 * (dx[-1] + dx[0])

911	                    b = b[:-1]

913	                    # Also, due to the periodicity, the system is not tri-diagonal.
914	                    # We need to compute a "condensed" matrix of shape (n-2, n-2).
915	                    # See https://web.archive.org/web/20151220180652/http://www.cfm.brown.edu/people/gk/chap6/node14.html
916	                    # for more explanations.
917	                    # The condensed matrix is obtained by removing the last column
918	                    # and last row of the (n-1, n-1) system matrix. The removed
919	                    # values are saved in scalar variables with the (n-1, n-1)
920	                    # system matrix indices forming their names:
921	                    a_m1_0 = dx[-2]  # lower left corner value: A[-1, 0]
922	                    a_m1_m2 = dx[-1]
923	                    a_m1_m1 = 2 * (dx[-1] + dx[-2])
924	                    a_m2_m1 = dx[-3]
925	                    a_0_m1 = dx[0]
926	
927	                    b[0] = 3 * (dxr[0] * slope[-1] + dxr[-1] * slope[0])
928	                    b[-1] = 3 * (dxr[-1] * slope[-2] + dxr[-2] * slope[-1])

930	                    Ac = A[:, :-1]
931	                    b1 = b[:-1]
932	                    b2 = np.zeros_like(b1)
933	                    b2[0] = -a_0_m1
934	                    b2[-1] = -a_m2_m1

936	                    # s1 and s2 are the solutions of (n-2, n-2) system
937	                    m = b1.shape[0]

939	                                      overwrite_b=False, check_finite=False)

941	
942	                    m = b2.shape[0]
943	                    s2 = solve_banded((1, 1), Ac, b2.reshape(m, -1), overwrite_ab=False,
944	                                      overwrite_b=False, check_finite=False)
945	                    s2 = s2.reshape(b2.shape)
946	
947	                    # computing the s[n-2] solution:
948	                    s_m1 = ((b[-1] - a_m1_0 * s1[0] - a_m1_m2 * s1[-1]) /
949	                            (a_m1_m1 + a_m1_0 * s2[0] + a_m1_m2 * s2[-1]))
950	
951	                    # s is the solution of the (n, n) system:
952	                    s = np.empty((n,) + y.shape[1:], dtype=y.dtype)
953	                    s[:-2] = s1 + s_m1 * s2
954	                    s[-2] = s_m1
955	                    s[-1] = s[0]

959	                        A[0, 1] = x[2] - x[0]
960	                        d = x[2] - x[0]

962	                                dxr[0]**2 * slope[1]) / d
963	                    elif bc_start[0] == 1:

965	                        A[0, 1] = 0
966	                        b[0] = bc_start[1]
967	                    elif bc_start[0] == 2:

969	                        A[0, 1] = dx[0]
970	                        b[0] = -0.5 * bc_start[1] * dx[0]**2 + 3 * (y[1] - y[0])
971	
972	                    if bc_end == 'not-a-knot':
973	                        A[1, -1] = dx[-2]
974	                        A[-1, -2] = x[-1] - x[-3]
975	                        d = x[-1] - x[-3]

977	                                 (2*d + dxr[-1])*dxr[-2]*slope[-1]) / d)
978	                    elif bc_end[0] == 1:
979	                        A[1, -1] = 1
980	                        A[-1, -2] = 0
981	                        b[-1] = bc_end[1]
982	                    elif bc_end[0] == 2:
983	                        A[1, -1] = 2 * dx[-1]
984	                        A[-1, -2] = dx[-1]
985	                        b[-1] = 0.5 * bc_end[1] * dx[-1]**2 + 3 * (y[-1] - y[-2])
986	
987	                    m = b.shape[0]
988	                    s = solve_banded((1, 1), A, b.reshape(m, -1), overwrite_ab=True,
989	                                     overwrite_b=True, check_finite=False)
990	                    s = s.reshape(b.shape)

992	        x, y, s = map(xp.asarray, (x, y, s))
993	        super().__init__(x, y, s, axis=0, extrapolate=extrapolate)
994	        self.axis = axis

996	    @staticmethod
997	    def _validate_bc(bc_type, y, expected_deriv_shape, axis):
998	        """Validate and prepare boundary conditions.
999	
1000	        Returns
1001	        -------
1002	        validated_bc : 2-tuple
1003	            Boundary conditions for a curve start and end.
1004	        y : ndarray
1005	            y casted to complex dtype if one of the boundary conditions has
1006	            complex dtype.
1007	        """
1008	        if isinstance(bc_type, str):
1009	            if bc_type == 'periodic':
1010	                if not np.allclose(y[0], y[-1], rtol=1e-15, atol=1e-15):
1011	                    raise ValueError(
1012	                        f"The first and last `y` point along axis {axis} must "
1013	                        "be identical (within machine precision) when "
1014	                        "bc_type='periodic'.")
1015	
1016	            bc_type = (bc_type, bc_type)
1017	
1018	        else:
1019	            if len(bc_type) != 2:
1020	                raise ValueError("`bc_type` must contain 2 elements to "
1021	                                 "specify start and end conditions.")
1022	
1023	            if 'periodic' in bc_type:
1024	                raise ValueError("'periodic' `bc_type` is defined for both "
1025	                                 "curve ends and cannot be used with other "
1026	                                 "boundary conditions.")
1027	
1028	        validated_bc = []
1029	        for bc in bc_type:
1030	            if isinstance(bc, str):
1031	                if bc == 'clamped':
1032	                    validated_bc.append((1, np.zeros(expected_deriv_shape)))
1033	                elif bc == 'natural':
1034	                    validated_bc.append((2, np.zeros(expected_deriv_shape)))
1035	                elif bc in ['not-a-knot', 'periodic']:
1036	                    validated_bc.append(bc)
1037	                else:
1038	                    raise ValueError(f"bc_type={bc} is not allowed.")
1039	            else:
1040	                try:
1041	                    deriv_order, deriv_value = bc
1042	                except Exception as e:
1043	                    raise ValueError(
1044	                        "A specified derivative value must be "
1045	                        "given in the form (order, value)."
1046	                    ) from e
1047	
1048	                if deriv_order not in [1, 2]:
1049	                    raise ValueError("The specified derivative order must "
1050	                                     "be 1 or 2.")
1051	
1052	                deriv_value = np.asarray(deriv_value)
1053	                if deriv_value.shape != expected_deriv_shape:
1054	                    raise ValueError(
1055	                        f"`deriv_value` shape {deriv_value.shape} is not "
1056	                        f"the expected one {expected_deriv_shape}."
1057	                    )
1058	
1059	                if np.issubdtype(deriv_value.dtype, np.complexfloating):
1060	                    y = y.astype(complex, copy=False)
1061	
1062	                validated_bc.append((deriv_order, deriv_value))
1063	
1064	        return validated_bc, y

22	class _Interpolator1D:
23	    """
24	    Common features in univariate interpolation
25	
26	    Deal with input data type and interpolation axis rolling. The
27	    actual interpolator can assume the y-data is of shape (n, r) where
28	    `n` is the number of x-points, and `r` the number of variables,
29	    and use self.dtype as the y-data type.
30	
31	    Attributes
32	    ----------
33	    _y_axis
34	        Axis along which the interpolation goes in the original array
35	    _y_extra_shape
36	        Additional trailing shape of the input arrays, excluding
37	        the interpolation axis.
38	    dtype
39	        Dtype of the y-data arrays. Can be set via _set_dtype, which
40	        forces it to be float or complex.
41	
42	    Methods
43	    -------
44	    __call__
45	    _prepare_x
46	    _finish_y
47	    _reshape_yi
48	    _set_yi
49	    _set_dtype
50	    _evaluate
51	
52	    """
53	
54	    __slots__ = ('_y_axis', '_y_extra_shape', 'dtype')
55	
56	    # generic type compatibility with scipy-stubs
57	    __class_getitem__: classmethod = classmethod(GenericAlias)
58	
59	    def __init__(self, xi=None, yi=None, axis=None):
60	        self._y_axis = axis
61	        self._y_extra_shape = None
62	        self.dtype = None
63	        if yi is not None:
64	            self._set_yi(yi, xi=xi, axis=axis)
65	
66	    def __call__(self, x):
67	        """
68	        Evaluate the interpolant
69	
70	        Parameters
71	        ----------
72	        x : array_like
73	            Point or points at which to evaluate the interpolant.
74	
75	        Returns
76	        -------
77	        y : array_like
78	            Interpolated values. Shape is determined by replacing
79	            the interpolation axis in the original array with the shape of `x`.
80	
81	        Notes
82	        -----
83	        Input values `x` must be convertible to `float` values like `int`
84	        or `float`.
85	
86	        """
87	        x, x_shape = self._prepare_x(x)
88	        y = self._evaluate(x)
89	        return self._finish_y(y, x_shape)
90	
91	    def _evaluate(self, x):
92	        """
93	        Actually evaluate the value of the interpolator.
94	        """
95	        raise NotImplementedError()
96	
97	    def _prepare_x(self, x):
98	        """Reshape input x array to 1-D"""
99	        x = _asarray_validated(x, check_finite=False, as_inexact=True)
100	        x_shape = x.shape
101	        return x.ravel(), x_shape
102	
103	    def _finish_y(self, y, x_shape):
104	        """Reshape interpolated y back to an N-D array similar to initial y"""
105	        y = y.reshape(x_shape + self._y_extra_shape)
106	        if self._y_axis != 0 and x_shape != ():
107	            nx = len(x_shape)
108	            ny = len(self._y_extra_shape)
109	            s = (list(range(nx, nx + self._y_axis))
110	                 + list(range(nx)) + list(range(nx+self._y_axis, nx+ny)))
111	            y = y.transpose(s)
112	        return y
113	
114	    def _reshape_yi(self, yi, check=False):
115	        yi = np.moveaxis(np.asarray(yi), self._y_axis, 0)
116	        if check and yi.shape[1:] != self._y_extra_shape:
117	            ok_shape = (f"{self._y_extra_shape[-self._y_axis:]!r} + (N,) + "
118	                        f"{self._y_extra_shape[:-self._y_axis]!r}")
119	            raise ValueError(f"Data must be of shape {ok_shape}")
120	        return yi.reshape((yi.shape[0], -1))
121	
122	    def _set_yi(self, yi, xi=None, axis=None):
123	        if axis is None:
124	            axis = self._y_axis
125	        if axis is None:
126	            raise ValueError("no interpolation axis specified")
127	
128	        yi = np.asarray(yi)
129	
130	        shape = yi.shape
131	        if shape == ():
132	            shape = (1,)
133	        if xi is not None and shape[axis] != len(xi):
134	            raise ValueError("x and y arrays must be equal in length along "
135	                             "interpolation axis.")
136	
137	        self._y_axis = (axis % yi.ndim)
138	        self._y_extra_shape = yi.shape[:self._y_axis] + yi.shape[self._y_axis+1:]
139	        self.dtype = None
140	        self._set_dtype(yi.dtype)
141	
142	    def _set_dtype(self, dtype, union=False):
143	        if np.issubdtype(dtype, np.complexfloating) \
144	               or np.issubdtype(self.dtype, np.complexfloating):
145	            self.dtype = np.complex128
146	        else:
147	            if not union or self.dtype != np.complex128:
148	                self.dtype = np.float64

152	    def derivatives(self, x, der=None):
153	        """
154	        Evaluate several derivatives of the polynomial at the point `x`.
155	
156	        Produce an array of derivatives evaluated at the point `x`.
157	
158	        Parameters
159	        ----------
160	        x : array_like
161	            Point or points at which to evaluate the derivatives
162	        der : int or list or None, optional
163	            How many derivatives to evaluate, or None for all potentially
164	            nonzero derivatives (that is, a number equal to the number
165	            of points), or a list of derivatives to evaluate. This number
166	            includes the function value as the '0th' derivative.
167	
168	        Returns
169	        -------
170	        d : ndarray
171	            Array with derivatives; ``d[j]`` contains the jth derivative.
172	            Shape of ``d[j]`` is determined by replacing the interpolation
173	            axis in the original array with the shape of `x`.
174	
175	        Examples
176	        --------
177	        >>> from scipy.interpolate import KroghInterpolator
178	        >>> KroghInterpolator([0,0,0],[1,2,3]).derivatives(0)
179	        array([1.0,2.0,3.0])
180	        >>> KroghInterpolator([0,0,0],[1,2,3]).derivatives([0,0])
181	        array([[1.0,1.0],
182	               [2.0,2.0],
183	               [3.0,3.0]])
184	
185	        """
186	        x, x_shape = self._prepare_x(x)
187	        y = self._evaluate_derivatives(x, der)
188	
189	        y = y.reshape((y.shape[0],) + x_shape + self._y_extra_shape)
190	        if self._y_axis != 0 and x_shape != ():
191	            nx = len(x_shape)
192	            ny = len(self._y_extra_shape)
193	            s = ([0] + list(range(nx+1, nx + self._y_axis+1))
194	                 + list(range(1, nx+1)) +
195	                 list(range(nx+1+self._y_axis, nx+ny+1)))
196	            y = y.transpose(s)
197	        return y

200	        """
201	        Evaluate a single derivative of the polynomial at the point `x`.
202	
203	        Parameters
204	        ----------
205	        x : array_like
206	            Point or points at which to evaluate the derivatives
207	
208	        der : int, optional
209	            Which derivative to evaluate (default: first derivative).
210	            This number includes the function value as 0th derivative.
211	
212	        Returns
213	        -------
214	        d : ndarray

221	        This may be computed by evaluating all derivatives up to the desired
222	        one (using self.derivatives()) and then discarding the rest.
223	
224	        """
225	        x, x_shape = self._prepare_x(x)
226	        y = self._evaluate_derivatives(x, der+1)
227	        return self._finish_y(y[der], x_shape)

247	        """

251	class KroghInterpolator(_Interpolator1DWithDerivatives):
252	    """Krogh interpolator (C∞ smooth).
253	
254	    The polynomial passes through all the pairs ``(xi, yi)``. One may
255	    additionally specify a number of derivatives at each point `xi`;
256	    this is done by repeating the value `xi` and specifying the
257	    derivatives as successive `yi` values.
258	
259	    Allows evaluation of the polynomial and all its derivatives.
260	    For reasons of numerical stability, this function does not compute
261	    the coefficients of the polynomial, although they can be obtained
262	    by evaluating all the derivatives.
263	
264	    Parameters
265	    ----------
266	    xi : array_like, shape (npoints, )
267	        Known x-coordinates. Must be sorted in increasing order.
268	    yi : array_like, shape (..., npoints, ...)
269	        Known y-coordinates. When an xi occurs two or more times in
270	        a row, the corresponding yi's represent derivative values. The length of `yi`
271	        along the interpolation axis must be equal to the length of `xi`. Use the
272	        `axis` parameter to select the correct axis.
273	    axis : int, optional
274	        Axis in the `yi` array corresponding to the x-coordinate values. Defaults to
275	        ``axis=0``.
276	
277	    Notes
278	    -----
279	    Be aware that the algorithms implemented here are not necessarily
280	    the most numerically stable known. Moreover, even in a world of
281	    exact computation, unless the x coordinates are chosen very
282	    carefully - Chebyshev zeros (e.g., cos(i*pi/n)) are a good choice -
283	    polynomial interpolation itself is a very ill-conditioned process
284	    due to the Runge phenomenon. In general, even with well-chosen
285	    x values, degrees higher than about thirty cause problems with
286	    numerical instability in this code.
287	
288	    Based on [1]_.
289	
290	    References
291	    ----------
292	    .. [1] Krogh, "Efficient Algorithms for Polynomial Interpolation
293	        and Numerical Differentiation", 1970.
294	
295	    Examples
296	    --------
297	    To produce a polynomial that is zero at 0 and 1 and has
298	    derivative 2 at 0, call
299	
300	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import KroghInterpolator
301	    >>> KroghInterpolator([0,0,1],[0,2,0])
302	
303	    This constructs the quadratic :math:`2x^2-2x`. The derivative condition
304	    is indicated by the repeated zero in the `xi` array; the corresponding
305	    yi values are 0, the function value, and 2, the derivative value.
306	
307	    For another example, given `xi`, `yi`, and a derivative `ypi` for each
308	    point, appropriate arrays can be constructed as:
309	
310	    >>> import numpy as np
311	    >>> rng = np.random.default_rng()
312	    >>> xi = np.linspace(0, 1, 5)
313	    >>> yi, ypi = rng.random((2, 5))
314	    >>> xi_k, yi_k = np.repeat(xi, 2), np.ravel(np.dstack((yi,ypi)))
315	    >>> KroghInterpolator(xi_k, yi_k)
316	
317	    To produce a vector-valued polynomial, supply a higher-dimensional
318	    array for `yi`:
319	
320	    >>> KroghInterpolator([0,1],[[2,3],[4,5]])
321	
322	    This constructs a linear polynomial giving (2,3) at 0 and (4,5) at 1.
323	
324	    """
325	
326	    def __init__(self, xi, yi, axis=0):
327	        super().__init__(xi, yi, axis)
328	
329	        self.xi = np.asarray(xi)
330	        self.yi = self._reshape_yi(yi)
331	        self.n, self.r = self.yi.shape
332	
333	        if (deg := self.xi.size) > 30:
334	            warnings.warn(f"{deg} degrees provided, degrees higher than about"
335	                          " thirty cause problems with numerical instability "
336	                          "with 'KroghInterpolator'", stacklevel=2)
337	
338	        c = np.zeros((self.n+1, self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
339	        c[0] = self.yi[0]
340	        Vk = np.zeros((self.n, self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
341	        for k in range(1, self.n):
342	            s = 0
343	            while s <= k and xi[k-s] == xi[k]:
344	                s += 1
345	            s -= 1
346	            Vk[0] = self.yi[k]/float_factorial(s)
347	            for i in range(k-s):
348	                if xi[i] == xi[k]:
349	                    raise ValueError("Elements of `xi` can't be equal.")
350	                if s == 0:
351	                    Vk[i+1] = (c[i]-Vk[i])/(xi[i]-xi[k])
352	                else:
353	                    Vk[i+1] = (Vk[i+1]-Vk[i])/(xi[i]-xi[k])
354	            c[k] = Vk[k-s]
355	        self.c = c
356	
357	    def _evaluate(self, x):
358	        pi = 1
359	        p = np.zeros((len(x), self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
360	        p += self.c[0,np.newaxis,:]
361	        for k in range(1, self.n):
362	            w = x - self.xi[k-1]
363	            pi = w*pi
364	            p += pi[:,np.newaxis] * self.c[k]
365	        return p
366	
367	    def _evaluate_derivatives(self, x, der=None):
368	        n = self.n
369	        r = self.r
370	
371	        if der is None:
372	            der = self.n
373	
374	        pi = np.zeros((n, len(x)))
375	        w = np.zeros((n, len(x)))
376	        pi[0] = 1
377	        p = np.zeros((len(x), self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
378	        p += self.c[0, np.newaxis, :]
379	
380	        for k in range(1, n):
381	            w[k-1] = x - self.xi[k-1]
382	            pi[k] = w[k-1] * pi[k-1]
383	            p += pi[k, :, np.newaxis] * self.c[k]
384	
385	        cn = np.zeros((max(der, n+1), len(x), r), dtype=self.dtype)
386	        cn[:n+1, :, :] += self.c[:n+1, np.newaxis, :]
387	        cn[0] = p
388	        for k in range(1, n):
389	            for i in range(1, n-k+1):
390	                pi[i] = w[k+i-1]*pi[i-1] + pi[i]
391	                cn[k] = cn[k] + pi[i, :, np.newaxis]*cn[k+i]
392	            cn[k] *= float_factorial(k)
393	
394	        cn[n, :, :] = 0
395	        return cn[:der]

399	    """Convenience function for Krogh interpolation.

401	    See `KroghInterpolator` for more details.

411	        Point or points at which to evaluate the derivatives.
412	    der : int or list or None, optional
413	        How many derivatives to evaluate, or None for all potentially
414	        nonzero derivatives (that is, a number equal to the number
415	        of points), or a list of derivatives to evaluate. This number
416	        includes the function value as the '0th' derivative.
417	    axis : int, optional
418	        Axis in the `yi` array corresponding to the x-coordinate values.
419	
420	    Returns
421	    -------
422	    d : ndarray
423	        If the interpolator's values are R-D then the
424	        returned array will be the number of derivatives by N by R.
425	        If `x` is a scalar, the middle dimension will be dropped; if
426	        the `yi` are scalars then the last dimension will be dropped.
427	
428	    See Also
429	    --------
430	    KroghInterpolator : Krogh interpolator
431	
432	    Notes
433	    -----
434	    Construction of the interpolating polynomial is a relatively expensive
435	    process. If you want to evaluate it repeatedly consider using the class
436	    KroghInterpolator (which is what this function uses).
437	
438	    Examples
439	    --------
440	    We can interpolate 2D observed data using Krogh interpolation:
441	
442	    >>> import numpy as np
443	    >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
444	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import krogh_interpolate
445	    >>> x_observed = np.linspace(0.0, 10.0, 11)
446	    >>> y_observed = np.sin(x_observed)
447	    >>> x = np.linspace(min(x_observed), max(x_observed), num=100)
448	    >>> y = krogh_interpolate(x_observed, y_observed, x)
449	    >>> plt.plot(x_observed, y_observed, "o", label="observation")
450	    >>> plt.plot(x, y, label="krogh interpolation")
451	    >>> plt.legend()
452	    >>> plt.show()
453	    """
454	
455	    P = KroghInterpolator(xi, yi, axis=axis)
456	    if der == 0:
457	        return P(x)
458	    elif _isscalar(der):
459	        return P.derivative(x, der=der)
460	    else:
461	        return P.derivatives(x, der=np.amax(der)+1)[der]
462

464	@xp_capabilities(out_of_scope=True)
465	def approximate_taylor_polynomial(f,x,degree,scale,order=None):
466	    """
467	    Estimate the Taylor polynomial of f at x by polynomial fitting.
468	
469	    .. deprecated:: 1.18.0
470	        This function is deprecated and will be removed in SciPy 1.20.0. Use the
471	        following code instead:
472	
473	        .. code-block:: python
474	
475	            import numpy as np
476	
477	            def f(z): return np.exp(z**2)  # example function
478	            N = 10  # number of terms in the Taylor expansion
479	            zz = np.exp(2j * np.pi * np.arange(N) / N)  # roots of unity
480	            c = np.fft.fft(f(zz)) / N
481	            c = np.real(c)  # c must be real by symmetry
482	
483	
484	    Parameters
485	    ----------
486	    f : callable
487	        The function whose Taylor polynomial is sought. Should accept
488	        a vector of `x` values.
489	    x : scalar
490	        The point at which the polynomial is to be evaluated.
491	    degree : int
492	        The degree of the Taylor polynomial
493	    scale : scalar
494	        The width of the interval to use to evaluate the Taylor polynomial.
495	        Function values spread over a range this wide are used to fit the
496	        polynomial. Must be chosen carefully.
497	    order : int or None, optional
498	        The order of the polynomial to be used in the fitting; `f` will be
499	        evaluated ``order+1`` times. If None, use `degree`.
500	
501	    Returns
502	    -------
503	    p : poly1d instance
504	        The Taylor polynomial (translated to the origin, so that
505	        for example p(0)=f(x)).
506	
507	    Notes
508	    -----
509	    The appropriate choice of "scale" is a trade-off; too large and the
510	    function differs from its Taylor polynomial too much to get a good
511	    answer, too small and round-off errors overwhelm the higher-order terms.
512	    The algorithm used becomes numerically unstable around order 30 even
513	    under ideal circumstances.
514	
515	    Choosing order somewhat larger than degree may improve the higher-order
516	    terms.
517	
518	    Examples
519	    --------
520	    We can calculate Taylor approximation polynomials of sin function with
521	    various degrees:
522	
523	    >>> import numpy as np
524	    >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
525	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import approximate_taylor_polynomial
526	    >>> x = np.linspace(-10.0, 10.0, num=100)
527	    >>> plt.plot(x, np.sin(x), label="sin curve")
528	    >>> for degree in np.arange(1, 15, step=2):
529	    ...     sin_taylor = approximate_taylor_polynomial(np.sin, 0, degree, 1,
530	    ...                                                order=degree + 2)
531	    ...     plt.plot(x, sin_taylor(x), label=f"degree={degree}")
532	    >>> plt.legend(bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left',
533	    ...            borderaxespad=0.0, shadow=True)
534	    >>> plt.tight_layout()
535	    >>> plt.axis([-10, 10, -10, 10])
536	    >>> plt.show()
537	
538	    """
539	    _warn_skips = (os.path.dirname(__file__),)
540	    msg = ("`approximate_taylor_polynomial` is deprecated and will be removed in "
541	           "SciPy 1.20.0.")
542	    warnings.warn(msg, DeprecationWarning, skip_file_prefixes=_warn_skips)
543	    if order is None:
544	        order = degree
545	
546	    n = order+1
547	    # Choose n points that cluster near the endpoints of the interval in
548	    # a way that avoids the Runge phenomenon. Ensure, by including the
549	    # endpoint or not as appropriate, that one point always falls at x
550	    # exactly.
551	    xs = scale*np.cos(np.linspace(0,np.pi,n,endpoint=n % 1)) + x
552	
553	    P = KroghInterpolator(xs, f(xs))
554	    d = P.derivatives(x,der=degree+1)
555	
556	    return np.poly1d((d/factorial(np.arange(degree+1)))[::-1])

559	class BarycentricInterpolator(_Interpolator1DWithDerivatives):
560	    r"""Barycentric (Lagrange with improved stability) interpolator (C∞ smooth).
561	
562	    Constructs a polynomial that passes through a given set of points.
563	    Allows evaluation of the polynomial and all its derivatives,
564	    efficient changing of the y-values to be interpolated,
565	    and updating by adding more x- and y-values. For numerical stability, a barycentric
566	    representation is used rather than computing the coefficients of the polynomial
567	    directly.
568	
569	
570	    Parameters
571	    ----------
572	    xi : array_like, shape (npoints, )
573	        1-D array of x-coordinates of the points the polynomial
574	        should pass through
575	    yi : array_like, shape (..., npoints, ...), optional
576	        N-D array of y-coordinates of the points the polynomial should pass through.
577	        If None, the y values will be supplied later via the `set_y` method.
578	        The length of `yi` along the interpolation axis must be equal to the length
579	        of `xi`. Use the ``axis`` parameter to select correct axis.
580	    axis : int, optional
581	        Axis in the yi array corresponding to the x-coordinate values. Defaults
582	        to ``axis=0``.
583	    wi : array_like, optional
584	        The barycentric weights for the chosen interpolation points `xi`.
585	        If absent or None, the weights will be computed from `xi` (default).
586	        This allows for the reuse of the weights `wi` if several interpolants
587	        are being calculated using the same nodes `xi`, without re-computation. This
588	        also allows for computing the weights explicitly for some choices of
589	        `xi` (see notes).
590	    rng : {None, int, `numpy.random.Generator`}, optional
591	        If `rng` is passed by keyword, types other than `numpy.random.Generator` are
592	        passed to `numpy.random.default_rng` to instantiate a ``Generator``.
593	        If `rng` is already a ``Generator`` instance, then the provided instance is
594	        used. Specify `rng` for repeatable interpolation.
595	
596	        If this argument `random_state` is passed by keyword,
597	        legacy behavior for the argument `random_state` applies:
598	
599	        - If `random_state` is None (or `numpy.random`), the `numpy.random.RandomState`
600	          singleton is used.
601	        - If `random_state` is an int, a new ``RandomState`` instance is used,
602	          seeded with `random_state`.
603	        - If `random_state` is already a ``Generator`` or ``RandomState`` instance then
604	          that instance is used.
605	
606	        .. versionchanged:: 1.15.0
607	            As part of the `SPEC-007 <https://scientific-python.org/specs/spec-0007/>`_
608	            transition from use of `numpy.random.RandomState` to
609	            `numpy.random.Generator` this keyword was changed from `random_state` to `rng`.
610	            For an interim period, both keywords will continue to work (only specify
611	            one of them). After the interim period using the `random_state` keyword will emit
612	            warnings. The behavior of the `random_state` and `rng` keywords is outlined above.
613	
614	    Notes
615	    -----
616	    This method is a variant of Lagrange polynomial interpolation [1]_ based on [2]_.
617	    Instead of using Lagrange's or Newton's formula, the polynomial is represented by
618	    the barycentric formula
619	
620	    .. math::
621	
622	        p(x) =
623	        \frac{\sum_{i=1}^m\ w_i y_i / (x - x_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^m w_i / (x - x_i)},
624	
625	    where :math:`w_i` are the barycentric weights computed with the general formula
626	
627	    .. math::
628	
629	        w_i = \left( \prod_{k \neq i} x_i - x_k \right)^{-1}.
630	
631	    This is the same barycentric form used by `AAA` and `FloaterHormannInterpolator`.
632	    However, in contrast, the weights :math:`w_i` are defined such that
633	    :math:`p(x)` is a polynomial rather than a rational function.
634	
635	    The barycentric representation avoids many of the problems associated with
636	    polynomial interpolation caused by floating-point arithmetic. However, it does not
637	    avoid issues that are intrinsic to polynomial interpolation. Namely, if the
638	    x-coordinates are equally spaced, then the weights can be computed explicitly using
639	    the formula from [2]_
640	
641	    .. math::
642	
643	        w_i = (-1)^i {n \choose i},
644	
645	    where :math:`n` is the number of x-coordinates. As noted in [2]_, this means that
646	    for large :math:`n` the weights vary by exponentially large factors, leading to the
647	    Runge phenomenon.
648	
649	    To avoid this ill-conditioning, the x-coordinates should be clustered at the
650	    endpoints of the interval. An excellent choice of points on the interval
651	    :math:`[a,b]` are Chebyshev points of the second kind
652	
653	    .. math::
654	
655	        x_i = \frac{a + b}{2} + \frac{a - b}{2}\cos(i\pi/n).
656	
657	    in which case the weights can be computed explicitly as
658	
659	    .. math::
660	
661	        w_i = \begin{cases}
662	                  (-1)^i/2 & i = 0,n \\
663	                  (-1)^i   & \text{otherwise}
664	              \end{cases}.
665	
666	    See [2]_ for more information. Note that for large :math:`n`, computing the weights
667	    explicitly (see examples) will be faster than the generic formula.
668	
669	    References
670	    ----------
671	    .. [1] https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lagrange_polynomial
672	    .. [2] Jean-Paul Berrut and Lloyd N. Trefethen, "Barycentric Lagrange
673	           Interpolation", SIAM Review 2004 46:3, 501-517
674	           :doi:`10.1137/S0036144502417715`
675	
676	    Examples
677	    --------
678	    To produce a quintic barycentric interpolant approximating the function
679	    :math:`\sin x`, and its first four derivatives, using six randomly-spaced
680	    nodes in :math:`(0, \pi/2)`:
681	
682	    >>> import numpy as np
683	    >>> import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
684	    >>> from scipy.interpolate import BarycentricInterpolator
685	    >>> rng = np.random.default_rng()
686	    >>> xi = rng.random(6) * np.pi/2
687	    >>> f, f_d1, f_d2, f_d3, f_d4 = np.sin, np.cos, lambda x: -np.sin(x), lambda x: -np.cos(x), np.sin
688	    >>> P = BarycentricInterpolator(xi, f(xi), random_state=rng)
689	    >>> fig, axs = plt.subplots(5, 1, sharex=True, layout='constrained', figsize=(7,10))
690	    >>> x = np.linspace(0, np.pi, 100)
691	    >>> axs[0].plot(x, P(x), 'r:', x, f(x), 'k--', xi, f(xi), 'xk')
692	    >>> axs[1].plot(x, P.derivative(x), 'r:', x, f_d1(x), 'k--', xi, f_d1(xi), 'xk')
693	    >>> axs[2].plot(x, P.derivative(x, 2), 'r:', x, f_d2(x), 'k--', xi, f_d2(xi), 'xk')
694	    >>> axs[3].plot(x, P.derivative(x, 3), 'r:', x, f_d3(x), 'k--', xi, f_d3(xi), 'xk')
695	    >>> axs[4].plot(x, P.derivative(x, 4), 'r:', x, f_d4(x), 'k--', xi, f_d4(xi), 'xk')
696	    >>> axs[0].set_xlim(0, np.pi)
697	    >>> axs[4].set_xlabel(r"$x$")
698	    >>> axs[4].set_xticks([i * np.pi / 4 for i in range(5)],
699	    ...                   ["0", r"$\frac{\pi}{4}$", r"$\frac{\pi}{2}$", r"$\frac{3\pi}{4}$", r"$\pi$"])
700	    >>> for ax, label in zip(axs, ("$f(x)$", "$f'(x)$", "$f''(x)$", "$f^{(3)}(x)$", "$f^{(4)}(x)$")):
701	    ...     ax.set_ylabel(label)
702	    >>> labels = ['Interpolation nodes', 'True function $f$', 'Barycentric interpolation']
703	    >>> axs[0].legend(axs[0].get_lines()[::-1], labels, bbox_to_anchor=(0., 1.02, 1., .102),
704	    ...               loc='lower left', ncols=3, mode="expand", borderaxespad=0., frameon=False)
705	    >>> plt.show()
706	
707	    Next, we show how using Chebyshev points of the second kind avoids the
708	    Runge phenomenon. In this example, we also compute the weights explicitly.
709	
710	    >>> n = 20
711	    >>> def f(x): return np.abs(x) + 0.5*x - x**2
712	    >>> i = np.arange(n)
713	    >>> x_cheb = np.cos(i*np.pi/(n - 1))  # Chebyshev points on [-1, 1]
714	    >>> w_i_cheb = (-1.)**i  # Explicit formula for weights of Chebyshev points
715	    >>> w_i_cheb[[0, -1]] /= 2
716	    >>> p_cheb = BarycentricInterpolator(x_cheb, f(x_cheb), wi=w_i_cheb)
717	    >>> x_equi = np.linspace(-1, 1, n)
718	    >>> p_equi = BarycentricInterpolator(x_equi, f(x_equi))
719	    >>> xx = np.linspace(-1, 1, 1000)
720	    >>> fig, ax = plt.subplots()
721	    >>> ax.plot(xx, f(xx), label="Original Function")
722	    >>> ax.plot(xx, p_cheb(xx), "--", label="Chebshev Points")
723	    >>> ax.plot(xx, p_equi(xx), "--", label="Equally Spaced Points")
724	    >>> ax.set(xlabel="$x$", ylabel="$f(x)$", xlim=[-1, 1])
725	    >>> ax.legend()
726	    >>> plt.show()
727	
728	    """ # numpy/numpydoc#87  # noqa: E501
729	
730	    @_transition_to_rng("random_state", replace_doc=False)
731	    def __init__(self, xi, yi=None, axis=0, *, wi=None, rng=None):
732	        super().__init__(xi, yi, axis)
733	
734	        rng = check_random_state(rng)
735	
736	        self.xi = np.asarray(xi, dtype=np.float64)
737	        self.set_yi(yi)
738	        self.n = len(self.xi)
739	
740	        # cache derivative object to avoid re-computing the weights with every call.
741	        self._diff_cij = None
742	
743	        if wi is not None:
744	            self.wi = wi
745	        else:
746	            # See page 510 of Berrut and Trefethen 2004 for an explanation of the
747	            # capacity scaling and the suggestion of using a random permutation of
748	            # the input factors.
749	            # At the moment, the permutation is not performed for xi that are
750	            # appended later through the add_xi interface. It's not clear to me how
751	            # to implement that and it seems that most situations that require
752	            # these numerical stability improvements will be able to provide all
753	            # the points to the constructor.
754	            self._inv_capacity = 4.0 / (np.max(self.xi) - np.min(self.xi))
755	            permute = rng.permutation(self.n, )
756	            inv_permute = np.zeros(self.n, dtype=np.int32)
757	            inv_permute[permute] = np.arange(self.n)
758	            self.wi = np.zeros(self.n)
759	
760	            for i in range(self.n):
761	                dist = self._inv_capacity * (self.xi[i] - self.xi[permute])
762	                dist[inv_permute[i]] = 1.0
763	                prod = np.prod(dist)
764	                if prod == 0.0:
765	                    raise ValueError("Interpolation points xi must be"
766	                                     " distinct.")
767	                self.wi[i] = 1.0 / prod
768	
769	    def set_yi(self, yi, axis=None):
770	        """
771	        Update the y values to be interpolated.
772	
773	        The barycentric interpolation algorithm requires the calculation
774	        of weights, but these depend only on the `xi`. The `yi` can be changed
775	        at any time.
776	
777	        Parameters
778	        ----------
779	        yi : array_like
780	            The y-coordinates of the points the polynomial will pass through.
781	            If None, the y values must be supplied later.
782	        axis : int, optional
783	            Axis in the `yi` array corresponding to the x-coordinate values.
784	
785	        """
786	        if yi is None:
787	            self.yi = None
788	            return
789	        self._set_yi(yi, xi=self.xi, axis=axis)
790	        self.yi = self._reshape_yi(yi)
791	        self.n, self.r = self.yi.shape
792	        self._diff_baryint = None
793	
794	    def add_xi(self, xi, yi=None):
795	        """
796	        Add more x values to the set to be interpolated.
797	
798	        The barycentric interpolation algorithm allows easy updating by
799	        adding more points for the polynomial to pass through.
800	
801	        Parameters
802	        ----------
803	        xi : array_like
804	            The x coordinates of the points that the polynomial should pass
805	            through.
806	        yi : array_like, optional
807	            The y coordinates of the points the polynomial should pass through.
808	            Should have shape ``(xi.size, R)``; if R > 1 then the polynomial is
809	            vector-valued.
810	            If `yi` is not given, the y values will be supplied later. `yi`
811	            should be given if and only if the interpolator has y values
812	            specified.
813	
814	        Notes
815	        -----
816	        The new points added by `add_xi` are not randomly permuted
817	        so there is potential for numerical instability,
818	        especially for a large number of points. If this
819	        happens, please reconstruct interpolation from scratch instead.
820	        """
821	        if yi is not None:
822	            if self.yi is None:
823	                raise ValueError("No previous yi value to update!")
824	            yi = self._reshape_yi(yi, check=True)
825	            self.yi = np.vstack((self.yi,yi))
826	        else:
827	            if self.yi is not None:
828	                raise ValueError("No update to yi provided!")
829	        old_n = self.n
830	        self.xi = np.concatenate((self.xi,xi))
831	        self.n = len(self.xi)
832	        self.wi **= -1
833	        old_wi = self.wi
834	        self.wi = np.zeros(self.n)
835	        self.wi[:old_n] = old_wi
836	        for j in range(old_n, self.n):
837	            self.wi[:j] *= self._inv_capacity * (self.xi[j]-self.xi[:j])
838	            self.wi[j] = np.multiply.reduce(
839	                self._inv_capacity * (self.xi[:j]-self.xi[j])
840	            )
841	        self.wi **= -1
842	        self._diff_cij = None
843	        self._diff_baryint = None
844	
845	    def __call__(self, x):
846	        """Evaluate the interpolating polynomial at the points x
847	
848	        Parameters
849	        ----------
850	        x : array_like
851	            Point or points at which to evaluate the interpolant.
852	
853	        Returns
854	        -------
855	        y : array_like
856	            Interpolated values. Shape is determined by replacing
857	            the interpolation axis in the original array with the shape of `x`.
858	
859	        Notes
860	        -----
861	        Currently the code computes an outer product between `x` and the
862	        weights, that is, it constructs an intermediate array of size
863	        ``(N, len(x))``, where N is the degree of the polynomial.
864	        """
865	        return _Interpolator1D.__call__(self, x)
866	
867	    def _evaluate(self, x):
868	        if x.size == 0:
869	            p = np.zeros((0, self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
870	        else:
871	            c = x[..., np.newaxis] - self.xi
872	            z = c == 0
873	            c[z] = 1
874	            c = self.wi / c
875	            with np.errstate(divide='ignore'):
876	                p = np.dot(c, self.yi) / np.sum(c, axis=-1)[..., np.newaxis]
877	            # Now fix where x==some xi
878	            r = np.nonzero(z)
879	            if len(r) == 1:  # evaluation at a scalar
880	                if len(r[0]) > 0:  # equals one of the points
881	                    p = self.yi[r[0][0]]
882	            else:
883	                p[r[:-1]] = self.yi[r[-1]]
884	        return p
885	
886	    def derivative(self, x, der=1):
887	        """
888	        Evaluate a single derivative of the polynomial at the point x.
889	
890	        Parameters
891	        ----------
892	        x : array_like
893	            Point or points at which to evaluate the derivatives
894	        der : int, optional
895	            Which derivative to evaluate (default: first derivative).
896	            This number includes the function value as 0th derivative.
897	
898	        Returns
899	        -------
900	        d : ndarray
901	            Derivative interpolated at the x-points. Shape of `d` is
902	            determined by replacing the interpolation axis in the
903	            original array with the shape of `x`.
904	        """
905	        x, x_shape = self._prepare_x(x)
906	        y = self._evaluate_derivatives(x, der+1, all_lower=False)
907	        return self._finish_y(y, x_shape)
908	
909	    def _evaluate_derivatives(self, x, der=None, all_lower=True):
910	        # NB: der here is not the order of the highest derivative;
911	        # instead, it is the size of the derivatives matrix that
912	        # would be returned with all_lower=True, including the
913	        # '0th' derivative (the undifferentiated function).
914	        # E.g. to evaluate the 5th derivative alone, call
915	        # _evaluate_derivatives(x, der=6, all_lower=False).
916	
917	        if (not all_lower) and (x.size == 0 or self.r == 0):
918	            return np.zeros((0, self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
919	
920	        if (not all_lower) and der == 1:
921	            return self._evaluate(x)
922	
923	        if (not all_lower) and (der > self.n):
924	            return np.zeros((len(x), self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
925	
926	        if der is None:
927	            der = self.n
928	
929	        if all_lower and (x.size == 0 or self.r == 0):
930	            return np.zeros((der, len(x), self.r), dtype=self.dtype)
931	
932	        if self._diff_cij is None:
933	            # c[i,j] = xi[i] - xi[j]
934	            c = self.xi[:, np.newaxis] - self.xi
935	
936	            # avoid division by 0 (diagonal entries are so far zero by construction)
937	            np.fill_diagonal(c, 1)
